speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Elsbeth Lewin, January 4, 1996, tape 4. I'd like to go back for just a moment. You had mentioned that the synagogue, the Haupt Synagogue, was, was set on fire for, on Kristallnacht but then dynamited. Can you just elaborate on that? Why was it dynamited and by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Nazis found that it didn't burn sufficiently. And it was, you know, as I said, a new building, relatively new and well constructed. It had a beautiful sort of flattish dome. And it-- the dome, it didn't collapse. It, it-- even though, you know-- it had strong windows, vaulted windows. And it didn't collapse. And the Nazis wanted to get rid of it all together. And so they dynamited it. You know, they tried to dynamite it. It, it didn't collapse even then. They had to, had to give it several tries. And even then, I guess, the rubble was, you know, pretty difficult to cart away. And there is now a sort of a part of a municipal building there. But they have recently, in the last few years, found some of the portico pillars of the, of the entrance to it and made a sort of a monument, sort of a memorial right there, you know, with a plaque and, and, and an inscription and so on. But that was just the entrance portico. I mean, one reason it probably didn't collapse is because so much of it was of pink marble and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they had to really, you know, blow it up. And for the longest time-- I, I went back there and picked up a little stone, or a little part of it, which I kept for the longest time as a memento. I may still have it someplace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we were very much attached to it, and it was, it was-- there was a certain splendor to it. There was a certain splendor to the whole life. I mean, you know, we-- many times, we, we, we-- when we think of, of the Holocaust and those times, we think of those terrible trains and, and, and the extermination camps and how the people looked so terrible then. And these were people who, before the war, had been just like you and me. They were totally part of the populace. They were ordinary citizens or, or distinguished citizens in some cases. And, you know, what Hitler essentially reduced them to were a non-people and, and, and just refugees and, and, and, and haunted, hunted people, as, as you know. And before the war, they had been solid citizens and lived in rather, you know, in some cases, very pleasant circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk about the kindertransport now. What were the kindertransports?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been organized by the British, in order to get young people out, and sometimes, you know, real children into safety. And the British had this, what they thought was a wonderful idea that, you know, they could save these children, not by giving money here and there, the way we do it these days, but by offering them homes in which they could live or schools in which they could live and bringing them up there until it blew over. I mean, it was bound to blow over sometime. And so many of us went on these kindertransports, which were essentially a collection of children who were brought on a train and then by ferry across the Channel and were delivered into sort of a central area, where they were then parceled out to people who would have them. And in some cases, it was a disaster because they were forced to give up their identities, and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the people, you know, people are people everywhere. They sometimes took advantage of them. Sometimes, you know, made them into servants or whatever and, and sometimes treated them extremely well. And I was lucky. I was-- I had been assigned to a school, which, of which some of the alumni had organized, through a wonderful woman called May Pickard, who was one of the alumni of the school called Culcheth Hall in Bowdon, Cheshire, who decided to take in five German Jewish girls. And they didn't have to be totally Jewish. They could be part Jewish because as far as Hitler was concerned, they were Jewish. And it was a girls' school which had run a little low on boarders. It was a boarding school. And I guess the people who made up that committee of, of alumni sponsored these girls and in a way helped their school out also at the same time. And, and when we got there, we were picked up. There were three of us from Mainz. And there were two other girls. And the three of us travelled together. And one of them was only about 11. And she was terribly homesick. She didn't speak a word of English. And she never saw her parents again. She did see her grandmother again, who was not Jewish, after the war. She's now living in South Africa. And I'm still in touch with her. Anyway, we, we were gathered up and taken to near Manchester, where this school was, in, in north of England. And they had uniforms, school uniforms waiting for us. In England, the schools wore uniforms, the school girls. And we were-- immediately, they tried to integrate us in the school as best they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1939. And it worked. It, it worked, in my case, certainly. I, I, you know, I stayed there for a year, which was essentially the school year. And my father, when he came to England, moved up into the area. He didn't stay in London, where he certainly would have been re-arrested as an enemy alien, which is what the British did. Even though they, they knew he, men were Jewish and, you know, they were afraid they were Germans. I mean, look what we did to the Japanese. And, and he moved to near where I was. As a matter of fact, he stayed at a YWCA, where he was a, a boarder, had little room there, and-- but I was sort of integrated into the school. I made a good friend-- English, couldn't be more English friend-- with whom I'm still in touch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everybody was extremely kind. In the, in the way-- I mean, everybody was kind. British boarding school system is-- has its own unkindnesses. But this was not part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you to go back a little bit, to take you back to Mainz just before you left. Do you recall the last night you spent as a family in Mainz and what that was like? And how did you get onto the kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- I, I don't recall the last night. I, I think my parents were much more conscious of the fact that we might never see each other again. And I, as I said, I, I thought it was an adventure. And I was not, I was not afraid. I was-- it didn't occur to me that I might never see my parents again. I don't know how I could have been so innocent, but-- and I did see them again, of course. But it, you know, it was just general excitement. It was more excitement, I mean, for me, certainly. And I recall my parents seeing me off and saying to my mother, don't cry. Don't cry. And going through Cologne, a cousin of my father's, you know-- I don't know how she found me, among all this, this train passing through, all those children on it. But suddenly there she was. She had come to say goodbye. And she didn't make it out, and neither-- they went to France. And their whole family perished. But I recall her being there. It was sort of a, a last farewell. And then I recall getting on the ferry in Holland and being seasick on the, on the Channel, and-- I really-- it's, it's really peculiar how my recollections of the Kristallnacht are absolutely clear. And, you know, I haven't forgotten a thing. Whereas, of this actual leaving, it's kind of hazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember being picked up in, in London by these ladies, who, you know, said, are you tired? And I said, what on earth is, are you tired? I mean, what language have I been studying? And it, it turned out she-- what she was saying is, are you tired. But they were very kind. And they, they got us to the schools, and-- yes? Do, do you know how you were chosen to be part of the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, there were friends of friends of my parents, who lived in England, who had somehow settled in England and had managed to get this family to come to England and, and also their children to, to come to England-- and, and, and-- because they knew this Mrs. Pickard, who had, who had organized this. I don't know how. I have-- again, when I was in Germany a couple of years ago, I met a nephew of theirs. And I still don't quite know just how this, how this all was organized. But they, I guess, told my-- you know, secondhand told my parents that here was an opportunity. If you are courageous enough to let your daughter go by herself, in order to save her, then here's an opportunity where she can go to a school and live a relatively normal life and, and so on. And also my parents weren't afraid of an English boarding school. One of my aunts had gone to one. And so they felt that this was a, you know, all in all, a kind, the kind of experience which, under normal circumstances, I might have had a couple of years later, just to be in an English boarding school, just to learn English or something like that. Of course, it's a different kind of an experience. But that's how I was picked. I-- you know, it seemed-- it was almost random.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you now find yourself in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I find myself in England, fortunately with my father not too far away, and suddenly learning everything in English. And fortunately, as I said also, I had had enough English so that, you know, by the time I got a little acclimatized, I, I did very well in England and, and made good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the question was, should I take what was then called School Certificate. I think it's now called A Levels. I don't-- I forget what it's called. But it's, it's sort of an entrance exam into the universities. And, and the, the big question was, should we stay there until I was finished with, with it, for which I'd been preparing all year? Or should we leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were strongly advised, correctly, that we should go when it was our turn to go. And I would have to attend-- otherwise I wouldn't be attending school in this country because I was, you know, formally finished with school. And we-- it was felt-- and I think I felt that, too-- that it would be of advantage to, to go to school, to go to high school in this country. And so my father and I went to Liverpool and boarded a Cunard liner there called the S-A-M-A-R-I-A. I think she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940, summer of 1940, May of 1940, probably. And first we went to, to London and saw the sights. And I had a cousin there, who had gotten out earlier. And, and, and then we went to Liverpool. In London, I guess we had to see the American consulate. And we had to go to Liverpool, which was fairly close to, to Manchester, and took the, the boat from there and zigzagged across the Atlantic, because there were U-boats in the Atlantic. And this was their way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was 10 days on the Atlantic. I was seasick again. I, I, I don't like ocean liners. [LAUGHS] And got here, and some cousins of my father's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] welcomed us into this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: What, what day and year was this? This was in May, 1940. And I don't know the exact date anymore. And we, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we stayed at, at this cousin's for a little while. And my father then took a room in a, in a, in an apartment, which was sort of a boarding house. And he got his meals. And, and he had a, a room to himself. And I got to stay with the son of a cousin of his. In other words, he was a second cousin. He had been born in this country. In fact, the father had been born in this country, and they had-- he had a wife and a, and a small, two-year-old child. And I was the babysitter for that whole, hot summer. It was hot. I didn't-- I'd never experienced heat like heat in, in New York before. And of course, there was no air conditioning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family that you stayed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gartner. G-A-R-T-N-E-R. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was not pleasant. It, it, it was sort of a basement apartment. And, and I was trying to prepare for some exams, school exams in, in math, which I couldn't have done, even if I had guidance. But I had no guidance. I just had the books. And I much rather read other things. And the kid was not nice. And the wife was not nice, either. And I was glad when I, you know, started going to school and had-- my father took an extra little room in that apartment for, for me. It was, it was barely a room. It was just large enough for a bed, but it-- I had a place to be. And, and meanwhile, my father, you know, knocked himself out to get, get my mother into this country. And if I may digress on that subject for a moment-- at the time, the Germans had overrun France and Holland and Belgium. And there was no way of getting out of Germany at that particular point, except to go through Russia and Japan. And it wasn't only Russia. It was Latvia and, and, and all this Central Asian countries and, and, and Korea and then finally Japan, where she stayed with a Jewish family for a couple of weeks until this boat left that she was able to take. Two things about it-- one thing was that they would pin a, a, a label on her, in Japanese, so that when she started going out on her own, she could always be directed back. And she rather enjoyed it. She was a woman in her 40s at the time, and she, you know, she was-- it was an adventure for her, too. The other thing was, though, that she hadn't been able to get out until, until the passage from Japan to the west coast was paid for. Up to Japan, she could pay for in German marks that she still had. The Japanese wanted American money. And there was no way of getting the $200 that he wanted to borrow. He had-- he came with debts. He owed my, my uncle, his, my mother's uncle money. He wanted to establish himself in this country. And there was just nobody who would, who would lend him the, the money. And he finally had to lower himself to the point where he asked a former suitor of my mother's who had been in the occupation army, the American occupation army in Mainz and had taken a shine to my mother before she was married. But my mother remembered his name. And this man lived in Boston. And my, my father-- actually, I don't know what it must have cost him to ask this man would he please, you know, lend him the money. And this man, after several correspondences, wrote back and said he had conferred with his friends, among whom was Ambassador Joseph Kennedy, about this problem. And they felt that it was not safe for a woman to travel alone through Russia and Siberia and Japan. And they could not recommended it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father-- to make, to cut it short, that was the end of that particular avenue. But my grandfather had had a business friend, a, a non-Jewish business friend, who had been in England at the time, who was a rich man, who had been in England at the time the war was declared. And he was himself interned on the Isle of Man as an enemy alien because he wasn't Jewish and he was German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father got in touch with him. I mean, he tried everything. And this man told him whom to go to, to ask for the money with, under, with his name. And that's how he got the money to get my, to get the passage for my mother. And my mother finally was, you know-- after spending this time in Kobe, Japan, went to-- took the ferry to Yokohama. And from there, got onto this boat called the and went to Seattle. And she-- and I have a, a newspaper clipping, copy of a newspaper clipping of how she arrived in Seattle and was interviewed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then went to San Francisco, where her whole family was, you know, who had been sponsored by this uncle. And called us up from San Francisco, said I'm here. And this is God's country. And they have white bread here. And why don't you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father said, I've just started on my first job. And the jobs are hard to come by. If you can-- if you think I can get a job in San Francisco, I'd be glad to come. And of course, there was no way my uncle-- my, my mother's brother there, who was a doctor was studying to take his exams over, in a foreign language. And so my mother got on the train. And she came to, to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to ask you a couple of questions. You said one-- a former suitor was part of the occupation forces. This is before the war. How did your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: That, that your mom, oh, that your mom met him when she was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother met him in, in, in Germany because, I mean, there was no, no ban on fraternizing with occupation forces, especially not American occupation forces. So you know, I mean, these young men were quartered in private homes. And so that, that, it was perfectly, you know, understandable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also you said she was interviewed when she got off the boat on the West Coast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who interviewed your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it was a, a newspaper interview. I, I have a copy of the newspaper with a, with a photograph. And she knew, I guess, more English than the rest of the people on the boat, and they, they, they, you know, this-- refugee ships did not come in every day in Seattle. So this was big news. And this was front-page news in Seattle. And so the reporters came, and they interviewed another family and, and my mother. And my mother was the one who spoke English. So she said, I, I am happy to be here. I have $6.18 to my name and, and a ticket to San Francisco. And I'll, I'll go on from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask her about the experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I think so. But, but she just said she was happy to be in the country. I don't think there was-- according to this newspaper clipping that I have, I don't think-- it was just taken for granted that she had, had, you know, that, that, she had to get out of Germany because it was an untenable situation there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people here were, in fact, aware of what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, people were aware of what was going on. They just didn't do anything about it, I mean, to, to save, to, to save people. The government didn't do anything. The government was very well aware of what was happening. The State Department was well aware of what was happening. They had meetings about it. And there was-- the American Jewish community put on no pressure. They, they were-- the American Jewish community felt we don't want to ruffle feathers. We don't want to rock the boat. There's enough anti-Semitism as there, as there is already. And we can't force anybody to do anything. There were people who were, who were trying. But it was-- President Roosevelt wasn't terribly interested. Mrs. Roosevelt was interested, but-- and all this we only found out, you know, fairly recently. I mean, we thought Roosevelt was god. But you know, he was, he was politics all the way. And so the American, the American government and the Americans didn't do anything. And they weren't pushed by the Jewish community to do anything. And so while it was common knowledge what was going on-- I mean, even, even after a while, the, the extermination camps were, became common knowledge. Because by that time, it was too late. But even then, the, the, the Allies wouldn't bomb the railway lines that were going to the, to the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever ask you about the concentration camps and what you knew about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was a child. I-- and, and anyway, we didn't know very much about them. It was-- the people who had been there wouldn't talk about it because they'd been told not to. And it was also too grisly. And so there was no-- I mean, there were people who knew about it. And there were Germans who were bound to know about it, even if they said they know nothing because they just lived close enough. I mean, there were enough people working there, and, and-- but officially, no, nobody ever asked me what I, you know, what I, I knew about it, or, or-- it was not expected of a, of a, of a child. I mean, I've told the story of, of the Kristallnacht. You know, that, that, that, you know, is what I personally knew and experienced. But on the other things I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, have we done this one already?
